name: Falcon 9 vs Long March 8
description: A side-by-side comparison of SpaceX's Falcon 9 and China's Long March 8, two medium-to-heavy orbital rockets from the United States and China, covering payload, reusability, status, and use cases.
summary: Falcon 9 and Long March 8 are both two-stage orbital rockets, but they sit in different payload classes and are at different stages on reusability. Falcon 9 is a partially reusable US rocket with roughly 22.8 t to LEO and a flight-proven first stage; Long March 8 is an expendable Chinese rocket with roughly 8 t to LEO, with a reusable variant still being developed.
keySimilarities: Both are two-stage orbital launch vehicles.
Both use kerosene/liquid-oxygen propulsion on the first stage.
Both are operated from coastal launch sites (Cape Canaveral / Wenchang).
keyDifferences: Falcon 9 is partially reusable today; Long March 8 is expendable in its current operational form.
Falcon 9 lifts roughly 2.9x more payload to LEO than Long March 8.
Falcon 9 is commercially operated by a private company; Long March 8 is a state program product.
useCases: Falcon 9: large commercial satellite constellations (e.g. Starlink), NASA cargo and crew, national-security payloads.
Long March 8: Chinese government and some commercial medium satellites to SSO and medium-inclination orbits.
limitations: Exact Long March 8 reusable-variant schedule is not publicly confirmed.
Listed payload figures depend on configuration and orbit; treat as nominal values.

faqs.question: Is Long March 8 reusable?
faqs.answer: The operational Long March 8 is expendable. A reusable first-stage variant has been flight-tested, but routine reuse is not yet in service.

faqs.question: Which rocket lifts more payload?
faqs.answer: Falcon 9 lifts roughly 22,800 kg to LEO versus about 8,000 kg for Long March 8, so Falcon 9 has the higher payload capacity.

faqs.question: Are they direct competitors?
faqs.answer: Not directly: Falcon 9 serves global commercial and US government markets, while Long March 8 primarily serves Chinese missions. They are comparable mainly as benchmarks of US and Chinese medium-lift capability.
